finding: Unresolved CMS placeholder syntax leaks into UI copy. The Buy form's Credit account block reads literally 'New [Precious Metal Account] will be created with your order'; the Review screen info banner reads 'A new [Precious Metals account] will be opened with this order. This account will hold your [paper gold] … under [Esolutions Alpha Pte Ltd]'.
novice_impact: Reads the brackets as design noise or as a placeholder yet-to-be-filled. Confidence in the bank's tooling drops at the exact moment they are about to commit money.
experienced_impact: Reads as an unfinished build. Will not commit a trade against UI that shows bracketed tokens. Hard credibility hit.
recommendation: Resolve every `[…]` token to its final string before launch. Add a lint step or pre-commit check that fails the build if any visible UI string still contains a bracketed placeholder pattern.

finding: Email templates ship with unresolved merge tokens visible in the design: `<Salutation>`, `<Surname>`, `<Name>`, `<Designation/rank>`, `<Department name>`. If any merge step fails in production the customer will receive these tokens verbatim.
novice_impact: Receives `Dear <Salutation> <Surname>` — immediately reads it as a phishing attempt or a system error and may distrust the entire email.
experienced_impact: Same — and corporate compliance will require any failed-merge case to be impossible.
recommendation: Personalise all six merge tokens at template-fill time; add a fail-closed check (do not send if any `<…>` token remains unresolved). Render a fallback like 'Dear Customer' if a personal name is unavailable.

finding: Entity-name mismatch on a regulated confirmation screen. The session header reads `ESOLUTIONS ALPHA PTE LTD` but the 'Your Precious Metals account has been opened' panel attributes the new account to `Esolutions Beta Pte Ltd`.
novice_impact: May believe the bank opened an account for the wrong entity. Will phone-in for verification and may dispute the trade.
experienced_impact: Same — and treasury / compliance will treat this as a real entity-confusion incident.
recommendation: Bind every entity reference on the acknowledgement to the session's customer-ID at render time. Add a contract test that asserts entity-name consistency between header context and any per-screen entity label.

finding: The Sell form supports a cross-currency settlement (XAU debit account → USD credit account) but shows no FX rate, no FX timing, no spread or markup disclosure. The user commits to a USD figure with no idea how the FX leg is priced.
novice_impact: Will not realise a second pricing decision (FX) is silently bundled into the trade. May be surprised by the eventual statement value.
experienced_impact: Cannot reconcile P&L or accounting without the FX rate. Likely to refuse the product for cross-currency settlement.
recommendation: Add a dedicated FX panel to Review whenever debit and credit currencies differ: 'XAU/SGD reference price, SGD/USD rate (with markup), all-in unit price, total USD'. Lock the FX quote with a countdown if appropriate.

finding: The form, Review, and Acknowledgement all reference 'Indicative' figures (Indicative price, Indicative market value, Investment amount derived from an indicative price). The order completes successfully, but the executed fill price is never explicitly stated anywhere — the acknowledgement repeats the indicative amount as if it were the fill.
novice_impact: Cannot tell whether they were filled at the displayed price or somewhere else. Will discover any slippage retrospectively in the statement.
experienced_impact: Cannot reconcile and will not trust the venue for repeat trades.
recommendation: On the acknowledgement screen and confirmation email, surface 'Executed unit price', 'Quantity', and 'Total paid' as the canonical record. Mark the on-chart figure 'Live indicative' to disambiguate.

finding: Review disclaimer says 'you confirm you have read, understood and agree to the Terms and Conditions' but the words 'Terms and Conditions' are not hyperlinked. The user has no in-flow access to the document they are agreeing to.
novice_impact: Click-through consent with no opportunity to read.
experienced_impact: Compliance-flag — no audit trail of 'document was made available'.
recommendation: Hyperlink 'Terms and Conditions' to the actual PDF/HTML doc. Track timestamp of the link-view per user. Also add a Key Fact Sheet link separately from the T&Cs.

finding: Account-creation-on-first-buy messaging is split across three places with three different phrasings: form ('New [Precious Metal Account] will be created with your order'), review banner ('A new [Precious Metals account] will be opened with this order'), acknowledgement panel ('Your Precious Metals account has been opened'). Inconsistent terminology for the same event.
novice_impact: Cognitive load — must reconcile three phrasings to confirm one event.
experienced_impact: Reads as careless copy work.
recommendation: Pick one canonical phrase ('A Precious Metals account will be opened') and use it in all three locations. Lift the message out of the Credit-account block on the form into a top-of-form info panel so it is impossible to miss.

finding: On the new-account-on-first-buy branch, the fact that completing this form will open a brand-new precious-metals account is communicated only inside the Credit-account block in plain text ('New [Precious Metal Account] will be created with your order'). The novice user can easily overlook that they are doing two things at once.
novice_impact: May believe they are merely placing an order; doesn't realise they are also opening a new account with its own statement, tax treatment, and reporting.
experienced_impact: Will spot it but expects this consequence to be flagged more prominently.
recommendation: Surface 'You will open a new Precious Metals account with this order' as a top-of-form info panel with an icon. Repeat on Review (already partially done) and Acknowledgement (already done).

finding: No risk disclosure or product-suitability statement surfaces anywhere in the captured flow. The disclaimer copy uses 'without recourse to Bank X' (legalese) without a plain-language counterbalance. Empty-state copy on the landing markets the product ('hedge against inflation, store of value') with no equivalent downside framing.
novice_impact: May invest without understanding that paper precious metals are non-capital-guaranteed and price can fall sharply.
experienced_impact: Notes the asymmetry between marketing prominence and risk prominence; treats it as a compliance gap.
recommendation: Add a persistent risk panel above the chart on Buy/Sell forms: 'Precious metal prices can fall as well as rise. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Holdings are unallocated paper bullion and are not SDIC-insured.' Plus a Key Fact Sheet link.

finding: 'Indicative' is used in at least four different ways without an inline definition: 'Indicative price (SGD/ounce)' on the chart, 'Indicative market value' in Holdings details, 'Market value (Indicative)' on the Sell form, 'Quantity to buy (Indicative)' on one Buy variant. Each carries a slightly different operational meaning.
novice_impact: Reads 'indicative' as a synonym for 'current'; misses that the executed price may differ.
experienced_impact: Knows the term but expects the bank to define it for compliance.
recommendation: Add an inline info-icon next to every 'Indicative' label opening a tooltip: 'For reference only. The price you transact at is set at execution and may differ.' Settle on one canonical noun phrase per concept.

finding: Acknowledgement copy reads 'Buy order for Gold (XAU) has been filled successfully' as a terminal state. Yet the global header has a 'Tasks and Statuses' pill with a red-dot indicator that typically signals pending second-eye approval in corporate banking. If a maker-checker step exists invisibly, novice users will believe the trade is final when it may not be.
novice_impact: Believes trade is complete; may take downstream actions (sell, allocate) based on a not-yet-final order.
experienced_impact: Will spot the ambiguity but cannot determine status from the screen alone.
recommendation: If maker-checker applies, change ack copy to 'Order submitted for approval. Awaiting Checker.' and only show 'Filled successfully' once both legs are completed. If single-control, explicitly state 'No further approval required — order is final.'

finding: Every in-app screen carries a 'Tasks and Statuses' utility pill in the global header — with a red-dot indicator suggesting pending items — but the overlay/page that opens from this pill is never shown in any captured frame. For a regulated corp-banking context where this pill likely hosts the maker-checker queue, that is critically under-signposted.
novice_impact: Doesn't know what 'Tasks and Statuses' means; ignores the badge.
experienced_impact: Knows it matters but cannot infer its current state from elsewhere.
recommendation: (a) Tooltip on hover explaining 'Tasks awaiting your approval'. (b) Open-pill drop-down designed and audited. (c) Surface pending counts in-context on the order/acknowledgement screens, not only in the global header.

finding: Recent orders table has no Status column. Rows show Metal / Order type / Quantity / Indicative price / Total / Date — but no Filled / Pending / Cancelled / Rejected / Awaiting-approval indicator.
novice_impact: Cannot tell whether a recent order has actually settled.
experienced_impact: Major operational gap — corp ops needs status for reconciliation.
recommendation: Add a STATUS column with coloured pills (Filled / Pending Approval / Awaiting Settlement / Cancelled / Rejected); make filterable.

finding: Acknowledgement gives no 'what happens next' copy beyond a single forward-looking line ('Future Precious Metals trades will settle into this account'). There is no settlement-cutoff time, no statement-availability time, no dispute-resolution path, no statement that an email has been dispatched, and no 'who else was notified'.
novice_impact: Doesn't know when to expect settlement / statement / email.
experienced_impact: Missing operational data needed to track the trade through to reconciliation.
recommendation: Add a short 'What happens next' panel on the ack: '(1) Email sent to <user>. (2) Settlement at end-of-day, SGT. (3) Statement available T+1. (4) Dispute path: …'

finding: The Sell form has a well-designed amber inline warning when the requested quantity exceeds available holdings ('Reduce the quantity to 25.86 ounces or less to proceed'). The Buy form has no equivalent insufficient-funds warning for the Debit account — the user can compose an over-budget order with no live feedback.
novice_impact: Trial-and-error their way into a server-side rejection.
experienced_impact: Mild annoyance — expects parity between Buy and Sell guardrails.
recommendation: Mirror the Sell's insufficient-holdings warning on the Buy form for insufficient-balance: 'Investment amount exceeds available balance (X SGD). Reduce the amount to proceed.' Use the same amber styling.

finding: The FAQ accordion containing 'Things to look out for', 'Fees and charges', 'Reason to invest' sits at the bottom of the landing page, below the fold. By the time a user clicks 'Trade Gold (XAU)' or 'Start trading', they have not seen fees or risks.
novice_impact: Buys without knowing the fee or the risk surface.
experienced_impact: Will scroll to find it, but treats burying these in a collapsed accordion as a regulatory weakness.
recommendation: Surface fee headline ('Fees from X% of trade value') and risk headline ('Capital at risk — paper bullion is not SDIC-insured') *above* the per-metal trade CTAs. Keep the full FAQ below.

finding: The Overview (empty) primary call-to-action 'Start trading' is rendered as a blue text link, while every other in-flow primary CTA is a slate filled button ('Review buy order', 'Confirm buy order', 'View order'). The most important action on the empty state has the weakest affordance.
novice_impact: May not realise 'Start trading' is the path forward; eye gets pulled to the metal cards instead.
experienced_impact: Mild friction.
recommendation: Replace the link with the standard slate primary button styled to match the rest of the product.

finding: The Confirm Buy/Sell order button is the only confirmation gate. There is no 2FA / SCA / hardware-token / soft-token / signing step between Review and order placement. For a real-money corporate trade, single-click confirmation is below industry norm.
novice_impact: Accidental clicks become orders.
experienced_impact: Compliance flag — corporate-banking trade execution typically requires a second authentication factor at the point of commit.
recommendation: Insert a 2FA / SCA / soft-token prompt between Review and Confirm. If maker-checker is in use, this remains required for the maker; the checker performs their own auth at their own step.

finding: On the collapsed ack, the expand/collapse affordance is a bare chevron with no text label. There is no 'Show details' or 'Hide details' wording.
novice_impact: May not realise more information is available.
experienced_impact: Mild friction.
recommendation: Add a text label alongside the chevron: 'Show details ▾' / 'Hide details ▴'. Keep the chevron for visual cue.

finding: Buy summary band reads 'Investment amount · Quantity to buy'; Sell summary band reads 'Quantity to sell · Market value'. The primary number flips position between Buy and Sell, and the SGD-amount field changes name from 'Investment amount' to 'Market value' even though it represents the cash leg in both cases.
novice_impact: Re-learn the layout per direction.
experienced_impact: Reads as careless copy work; questions whether the values truly mean the same thing.
recommendation: Use a single label per direction — 'Cost (SGD)' for Buy, 'Proceeds (SGD)' for Sell — and place it in the same position in both summary bands.

finding: Numeric figures in the Holdings details and Recent orders tables use a proportional sans (not tabular figures). Digits across rows do not line up vertically — decimals drift, making column scanning slower than necessary for a finance product.
novice_impact: Slower scanning, but readable.
experienced_impact: Recognised as substandard finance-table styling.
recommendation: Apply `font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums` to every numeric column in the Holdings, Recent orders, and acknowledgement summaries. Right-align numerics.

finding: The primary navigation label is inconsistent across the design: most screens read 'FX and Investment'; the Buy/Sell trade form variants render the same nav item as 'FX and Treasury'. Same global nav, two labels for the same item.
novice_impact: Quiet confusion — 'is this the same product?'
experienced_impact: Spotted immediately and read as a build defect.
recommendation: Pick one — recommend 'FX and Investment' since that's used on the customer-facing surfaces — and unify across every screen.
